content.full_text:
December 17, 1993


Honorable William J. "Pete" Knight
Member, California State Assembly
State Capitol
P.O. Box 942849
Sacramento, CA  94249-0001

                                    Re:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
                                         Our File No. I-93-466

Dear Assemblymember Knight:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding your responsibilities under Section 89001 of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹  Since you are seeking general
assistance and not inquiring about a specific situation, your
letter is considered a request for informal assistance.²

                                QUESTION

     Would it be a violation of Section 89001 (the "mass mailing"
provisions of the Act) for you to use public funds to purchase and
distribute a directory which includes the names, titles, and
photographs of California's state and federal elected officials
and state appointed officials?

                                CONCLUSION

     It would not be a violation of Section 89001 to use public
funds to pay for the directory provided that no more than 200
directories are sent in a calendar month.  However, depending on

_____________________
  1  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000 - 18954.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

  2  Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA  95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-93-466
Page 2


the circumstances surrounding the use of the directory, the
payment for them could constitute a reportable contribution or a
misuse of public funds. We do not have sufficient facts to make
this determination.

FACTS

You wish to purchase, with state funds, 500 copies of a
government directory which will include California's Governor,
constitutional officers, members of the State Senate and Assembly,
and congressional members. The directory is a pictorial guide
which will also include their government addresses, telephone
numbers, occupations, and political jurisdiction.

On the front cover of the directory, and on the inside of the
directory, your black and white picture will be displayed. The
front cover will also include your name and the official seal of
the California State Assembly.

The directory will be mailed or picked-up at your legislative
offices, or it will be provided in informational materials given
to school-aged children and other constituents who visit the State
Capitol.

ANALYSIS

Mass Mailings

In June 1988 Proposition 73 amended Section 89001 of the Act
to provide: "No newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at
public expense." The Commission adopted Regulation 18901 to
clarify which mailings were permissible and which were prohibited
under the Act.³

Regulation 18901(a) provides that a mailing is prohibited
only if all of the following apply:

(1) Any item sent is delivered, by any means,
to the recipient at his or her residence, place of
employment or business, or post office box. For
purposes of this subdivision (a)(1), the item
delivered to the recipient must be a tangible item,
such as a videotape, record, or button, or a
written document.


³ The Commission's authority to interpret Section 89001 to avoid
the absurd results of its literal application was upheld in
Watson v. Fair Political Practices Com. (1990) 217 Cal.App.3d
1059.

Our File No. I-93-466
Page 3


(2)  The item sent either:

(A)  Features an elected officer
affiliated with the agency which produces or
sends the mailing, or

(B)  Includes the name, office,
photograph, or other reference to an elected
officer affiliated with the agency which
produces or sends the mailing, and is prepared
or sent in cooperation, consultation,
coordination, or concert with the elected
officer.

(3)(A)  Any of the costs of distribution is
paid for with public moneys; or

(B)  Costs of design, production, and
printing exceeding $50.00 are paid with public
moneys, and the design, production, or
printing is done with the intent of sending
the item other than as permitted by this
regulation.

(4)  More than two hundred substantially
similar items are sent, in a single calendar month,
excluding any item sent in response to an
unsolicited request and any item described in
subdivision (b).

Regulation 18901(a).

Thus, a mass mailing is prohibited if all of the factors set
out in Regulation 18901(a) are met. If the factors are not met,
the mailing is not a mass mailing.

1.  Inclusion of a Reference to an Elected Officer

If the item features an elected officer, or includes the
name, photograph or any reference to an elected officer who is
affiliated with the agency that produces or distributes the
mailing, the item is subject to the restrictions of the
regulation. (Regulation 18901(a)(2).)

As a member of the California State Assembly, you are an
elected officer affiliated with the California State Assembly.4
The directory, which you intend to distribute through your
legislative offices, will include your photograph on the front
cover, and it will include a second photograph on the inside of

4
We assume that the source of public funds would be the
California State Assembly.

Our File No. I-93-466
Page 4


the directory. Therefore, the item will clearly feature5 you as
an elected officer affiliated with the agency that sends the
mailing. In addition, the directory will include your name and
other identifying information. Therefore, this factor is met.

     2. Public Moneys

     Section 89001 provides that no newsletter or other mass
mailing shall be sent at public expense. Regulation 18901(a)(3)
specifies that a mass mailing is "sent at public expense" within
the meaning of Section 89001 if either the costs of distribution
are paid for with public moneys, or more than $50 in public money
is paid for the costs of design or production, and the design or
printing is done with the intent of sending the item other than
permitted under the regulation.

     Public funds will be used to purchase and distribute the
directories. Therefore, this requirement is also met.

     3. Delivery

     Regulation 18901(a)(1) restricts items that are mailed or
delivered, by any means, to a person's home, office, or post
office box. (Anaya Advice Letter, No. A-91-215.) Therefore, this
factor is met with respect to the copies of the directory which
exceed 200 and which are delivered through your capitol or
district offices to a person's home, office, or post office box.

     You indicate that the directories may be provided to school-
aged children who tour the State Capitol. If the directories are
provided to the children for delivery to the parents at their
home, a mass mailing would have been sent for purposes of
Regulation 18901.

     The directories may also be picked up at one of your
legislative offices. The copies of the directory that are set out
for the public to pick up on their own, or are handed out in a
public area, are not subject to the restrictions of Regulation
18901. Therefore, these items would be exempt from the
restrictions of Regulation 18901 and would not count toward the
200.

     Please note, however, that where an item would otherwise fall
within the restrictions of Regulation 18901 because the other
factors are met, you should be cautious that the item is not
redistributed by one of the prohibited means. For example, the

5    An elected officer is "featured" when an item mailed includes
the elected officer's photograph or signature, or singles out the
elected officer by the manner of display of his or her name or
office in the layout of the document, such as by headlines,
captions, type size, typeface, or type color. (Regulation
18901(c)(2).)

Our File No. I-93-466
Page 5


directories could not be utilized as an enclosure in any mass
mailing or, as noted above, redistributed to persons at their
home, places of employment, or post office box. (Cornelius Advice
Letter, No. I-92-592; Lavagette Advice Letter No. A-90-199.)

4. Mass Mailings

Finally, Regulation 18901 applies only to a "mass mailing" as
defined in the Act. "Mass mailing" means over two hundred
substantially similar pieces of mail, but does not include any mail
which is sent in response to an unsolicited request, letter or
other inquiry, or any items that fall within the exceptions set
forth in Regulation 18901(b). (Section 82041.4.)

An "unsolicited" request is defined, in part, as follows:

A written or oral communication (including a
petition) which specifically requests a response
and which is not requested or induced by the
recipient elected officer or by any third person
acting at his or her behest. However, an
unsolicited oral or written communication (includ-
ing a petition) which contains no specific request
for a response, will be deemed to constitute an
unsolicited request for a single written response.

Regulation 18901(c)(4)(A).

Thus, copies of the directory distributed to persons who have
submitted written requests to receive the directory through the
mail will not fall under the restrictions of Section 89001.

Also, please note that the use of the directory by a public
official seeking re-election may be deemed a contribution from the
California State Assembly to the public official. (Section
82015.) If this is the case, the contribution may qualify the
public agency that pays for the directories as a committee and
could require the disclosure of campaign forms.⁶ (Regulation
18420.)

Furthermore, Section 85300 provides that no public officer
shall expend and no candidate shall accept any public moneys for
the purpose of seeking elective office. Absent specific facts

⁶
Should the directory be used for campaign-related purposes,
there may be the additional question of whether such an
expenditure would be considered a legal expenditure. (See, e.g.
Penal Code Section 424 Stanson v. Mott (1976) 17 Cal. 3d 206;
People v. Sperl (1976) 54 Cal. App. 3d 640; and People v. Battin
(1978) 77 Cal. App. 3d 635.) Penal Code Section 424 which deals
with the misuse of public moneys, are outside the jurisdiction of
the Commission. Penal Code Section 424 is interpreted and
enforced by the district attorney and the Attorney General.

Our File No. I-93-466
Page 6


concerning the contents and use of the directories, we cannot
determine whether their purchase with public funds would
constitute a contribution under the Political Reform Act, or
whether Section 85300 is applicable.

I trust this letter addresses your concerns. If you have any
further questions regarding this matter, please feel free to
contact me at (916) 322-5901.7

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division




7 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
